name.es: Pain brié (pan de los marineros normandos)

description.en: A dense Norman loaf whose dough is beaten and worked far longer than usual, 'brié' means hammered, to crush out air and leave an extremely tight crumb. Butter keeps it tender, and it once fed fishermen for weeks at sea.
description.de: Ein dichtes normannisches Brot, dessen Teig weit länger als üblich geschlagen und bearbeitet wird, 'brié' bedeutet gehämmert, um Luft auszutreiben und eine extrem feste Krume zu erhalten. Butter hält es zart; einst ernährte es Fischer wochenlang auf See.
description.es: Una hogaza normanda densa cuya masa se golpea y trabaja mucho más de lo habitual, 'brié' significa martillado, para expulsar el aire y dejar una miga extremadamente prieta. La mantequilla la mantiene tierna; antaño alimentaba a los pescadores semanas enteras en el mar.
description.fr: Un pain normand dense dont la pâte est longuement battue, 'brié' signifie martelé, pour en chasser l'air et obtenir une mie extrêmement serrée. Le beurre le garde moelleux ; il nourrissait autrefois les marins-pêcheurs des semaines en mer.

origin.note: Recorded as early as a 1567 decree under the name 'pain de Chapître'; prized by Norman fishermen and sailors for keeping fresh for one to two weeks at sea.

ingredients.note: T55, split between the pre-ferment and final dough

keeping.note: Famed for keeping up to two weeks, the whole reason Norman sailors carried it to sea.

summary.en: Mix part of the flour with water and yeast (locally still called the 'levain', though it's yeasted); let rise about 2 hours.
Combine the remaining flour, water, yeast and butter into a rough paste.
Bring everything together with the salt and knead vigorously by hand for a full 15-20 minutes, this hard, sustained work is what tightens ('brie') the crumb.
Rest briefly, then divide and shape into stubby loaves.
Cut parallel scores lengthwise and proof about an hour.
Bake until a pale golden 'blonde' crust forms; cool wrapped in cloth to keep the crumb soft.

source.adaptation: Salt increased from the source's 10g to 23g (1.8%) to match standard French bread-salting practice; source's own figure ran unusually low.
